title: 31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies
text:
31.5 Resource Optimization

31.5.1 Batch Processing
# src/cost/batch_processor.py

class BatchProcessor:
    def __init__(self, model_client, batch_size=10):
        self.model_client = model_client
        self.batch_size = batch_size
    
    def process_batch(self, prompts: List[str]) -> List[str]:
        """Process multiple prompts in a batch."""
        results = []
        
        for i in range(0, len(prompts), self.batch_size):
            batch = prompts[i:i + self.batch_size]
            batch_results = self.model_client.generate_batch(batch)
            results.extend(batch_results)
        
        return results
    
    def estimate_savings(self, n_requests: int) -> Dict:
        """Estimate cost savings from batching."""
        # Individual processing cost
        individual_cost = n_requests * 0.002  # Example cost per request
        
        # Batch processing cost
        n_batches = (n_requests + self.batch_size - 1) // self.batch_size
        batch_cost = n_batches * 0.01  # Example cost per batch
        
        savings = individual_cost - batch_cost
        savings_percent = (savings / individual_cost * 100) if individual_cost > 0 else 0
        
        return {
            'individual_cost': individual_cost,
            'batch_cost': batch_cost,
            'savings': savings,
            'savings_percent': savings_percent
        }


31.5.2 Token Optimization
# src/cost/token_optimizer.py

class TokenOptimizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.optimizations = []
    
    def optimize_prompt(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        """Optimize prompt to reduce token count."""
        # Remove redundant whitespace
        optimized = ' '.join(prompt.split())
        
        # Remove unnecessary instructions
        optimized = self.remove_redundant_instructions(optimized)
        
        # Use shorter formulations
        optimized = self.use_shorter_formulations(optimized)
        
        return optimized
    
    def remove_redundant_instructions(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        """Remove redundant instructions from prompt."""
        # This would use pattern matching or model-based detection
        return prompt
    
    def use_shorter_formulations(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        """Replace long phrases with shorter equivalents."""
        replacements = {
            'please provide': 'give',
            'in order to': 'to',
            'as well as': 'and',
            'for the purpose of': 'for'
        }
        
        for old, new in replacements.items():
            prompt = prompt.replace(old, new)
        
        return prompt
    
    def estimate_token_savings(self, original: str, optimized: str) -> Dict:
        """Estimate token savings."""
        original_tokens = len(original.split())
        optimized_tokens = len(optimized.split())
        
        savings = original_tokens - optimized_tokens
        savings_percent = (savings / original_tokens * 100) if original_tokens > 0 else 0
        
        return {
            'original_tokens': original_tokens,
            'optimized_tokens': optimized_tokens,
            'savings': savings,
            'savings_percent': savings_percent
        }


31.5.3 Context Window Optimization
# src/cost/context_optimizer.py

class ContextOptimizer:
    def __init__(self, max_context_length=3000):
        self.max_context_length = max_context_length
    
    def optimize_context(self, documents: List[Dict], 
                        query: str) -> str:
        """Optimize context for query."""
        # Rank documents by relevance
        ranked = self.rank_documents(documents, query)
        
        # Select top documents within context limit
        selected = []
        current_length = 0
        
        for doc in ranked:
            doc_text = f"[{doc['source']}]\n{doc['content']}\n\n"
            
            if current_length + len(doc_text) > self.max_context_length:
                break
            
            selected.append(doc_text)
            current_length += len(doc_text)
        
        return ''.join(selected)
    
    def rank_documents(self, documents: List[Dict], 
                      query: str) -> List[Dict]:
        """Rank documents by relevance to query."""
        # This would use semantic similarity or keyword matching
        # For now, return as-is
        return documents

31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies

title: 31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies
text:
31.7 Real-World Optimization Patterns

31.7.1 Pattern 1: Tiered Model Strategy
Use different models for different complexity levels:
# src/patterns/tiered_models.py

class TieredModelStrategy:
    def __init__(self, model_router):
        self.model_router = model_router
    
    def execute(self, input_data: Dict) -> Dict:
        """Execute with tiered model strategy."""
        # Assess complexity
        complexity = self.assess_complexity(input_data)
        
        # Route to appropriate tier
        if complexity == 'low':
            model = 'gpt-3.5-turbo'
        elif complexity == 'medium':
            model = 'gpt-4-turbo'
        else:
            model = 'gpt-4'
        
        # Execute
        result = self.model_router.execute_with_routing(
            input_data,
            default_model=model
        )
        
        return result
    
    def assess_complexity(self, input_data: Dict) -> str:
        """Assess task complexity."""
        prompt = input_data['prompt']
        
        # Simple heuristics
        if len(prompt) < 100:
            return 'low'
        elif len(prompt) < 500:
            return 'medium'
        else:
            return 'high'


31.7.2 Pattern 2: Progressive Refinement
Start with cheap model, refine with expensive model if needed:
# src/patterns/progressive_refinement.py

class ProgressiveRefinement:
    def __init__(self, cheap_client, expensive_client, quality_threshold=0.7):
        self.cheap_client = cheap_client
        self.expensive_client = expensive_client
        self.quality_threshold = quality_threshold
    
    def execute(self, input_data: Dict) -> Dict:
        """Execute with progressive refinement."""
        # Try cheap model first
        cheap_result = self.cheap_client.generate(input_data['prompt'])
        cheap_quality = self.assess_quality(cheap_result)
        
        if cheap_quality >= self.quality_threshold:
            return {
                'output': cheap_result,
                'model': 'cheap',
                'quality': cheap_quality,
                'refined': False
            }
        
        # Refine with expensive model
        refined_result = self.expensive_client.generate_with_system(
            "Improve this response:",
            cheap_result
        )
        
        return {
            'output': refined_result,
            'model': 'expensive',
            'quality': self.assess_quality(refined_result),
            'refined': True
        }
    
    def assess_quality(self, output: str) -> float:
        """Assess output quality."""
        # This would use evaluation metrics
        return 0.8  # Placeholder


31.7.3 Pattern 3: Adaptive Caching
Adjust caching strategy based on usage patterns:
# src/patterns/adaptive_cache.py

class AdaptiveCache:
    def __init__(self, base_cache):
        self.base_cache = base_cache
        self.access_patterns = {}
    
    def get(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> Optional[str]:
        """Get from cache with adaptive strategy."""
        result = self.base_cache.get(prompt, model)
        
        # Track access pattern
        key = self.base_cache.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        if key not in self.access_patterns:
            self.access_patterns[key] = 0
        self.access_patterns[key] += 1
        
        return result
    
    def should_cache(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> bool:
        """Determine if response should be cached."""
        # Cache if prompt is likely to be repeated
        key = self.base_cache.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        access_count = self.access_patterns.get(key, 0)
        
        # Cache if accessed more than once
        return access_count > 0

31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies

title: 31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies
text:
31.8 Best Practices

31.8.1 1. Monitor Costs Continuously
Track costs in real-time and set up alerts for budget overruns.


31.8.2 2. Optimize at Multiple Levels
Optimize costs at the model, infrastructure, and application levels.


31.8.3 3. Use Caching Strategically
Implement multi-level caching with appropriate cache invalidation strategies.


31.8.4 4. Choose the Right Model
Select models based on task requirements, not just capabilities.


31.8.5 5. Optimize Prompts
Reduce token count through prompt optimization without sacrificing quality.


31.8.6 6. Batch When Possible
Use batch processing for high-volume operations.


31.8.7 7. Regular Review
Regularly review costs and identify optimization opportunities.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies

title: 31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies
text:
31.9 Common Pitfalls

31.9.1 1. Over-Optimization
Problem: Sacrificing quality to reduce costs.
Solution: Maintain quality thresholds while optimizing.


31.9.2 2. Ignoring Hidden Costs
Problem: Focusing only on model costs, ignoring infrastructure and operations.
Solution: Consider total cost of ownership.


31.9.3 3. Stale Caching
Problem: Using outdated cached responses.
Solution: Implement proper cache invalidation strategies.


31.9.4 4. Inadequate Monitoring
Problem: Not tracking costs until they become problematic.
Solution: Implement cost monitoring from the start.


31.9.5 5. Premature Optimization
Problem: Optimizing before understanding usage patterns.
Solution: Monitor usage first, then optimize based on data.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies

title: 31  Chapter 26 — Cost Optimization Strategies
text:
31.10 Conclusion
Cost optimization is a critical discipline in AI engineering. By understanding cost sources, implementing intelligent model routing, using multi-level caching, optimizing resources, and monitoring continuously, engineers can build efficient, scalable AI systems that deliver value while controlling costs.
The strategies in this chapter—model selection and routing, caching techniques, resource optimization, and real-time monitoring—provide a practical toolkit for managing AI system costs without sacrificing quality or performance.
With these techniques, AI engineers can balance the three fundamental constraints of cost, latency, and reliability, building systems that are not only powerful but also efficient and sustainable.
This concludes the Practical Guide to AI Engineering. Together with the theoretical foundations established in earlier chapters, these practical techniques provide a complete framework for designing, building, optimizing, and maintaining intelligent systems at scale.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.1 Introduction
This book has explored AI engineering from multiple perspectives: the fundamental shift from deterministic to probabilistic systems, the layered architecture of the AI engineering stack, the practical techniques for building systems, and the strategies for optimization and production.
Yet the true power of this knowledge lies not in understanding each component in isolation, but in grasping how they fit together into a coherent whole. This chapter provides the essential framework that every AI engineer must internalize—the skeleton that gives structure to everything else.
If you remember nothing else from this book, remember this framework.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
section: 32.2 The AI Engineering Stack: The Complete Picture
text:
32.2 The AI Engineering Stack: The Complete Picture
At its core, AI engineering is organized around three architectural domains:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         System Layer                            │
│  pipelines, evaluation, infrastructure          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Control Layer                           │
│  prompts, tools, agents                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Model Layer                             │
│  models, data                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
This is not just a diagram—it is a mental model for thinking about AI systems.

32.2.1 The Three Domains
Model Layer provides raw intelligence through trained models and the data that shapes them.
Control Layer directs that intelligence through prompts, tools, and agents.
System Layer organizes everything into reliable, scalable applications.
Every AI system you build will have components from each layer. Every architectural decision you make will involve trade-offs across these layers. Every problem you encounter will have its roots in one or more of these layers.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.3 The Eight Layers: Detailed Breakdown
Within the three domains lie eight specific layers, each with clear responsibilities:
1. Model
2. Data
3. Prompt
4. Tool
5. Agent
6. Pipeline
7. Evaluation
8. Infrastructure

32.3.1 Model Layer Details
Model: - Large language models, embedding models, vision models - Provides probabilistic inference - Cannot operate effectively alone - Requires direction through prompts and context
Data: - Training data shapes model capabilities - Contextual data provides runtime information - Data quality directly affects system performance - Retrieval mechanisms expand model knowledge


32.3.2 Control Layer Details
Prompt: - Interface between deterministic systems and probabilistic models - Specifies task, context, constraints, format - Functions as architectural component, not just text - Can be parameterized, versioned, and optimized
Tool: - Extends model capabilities beyond internal knowledge - APIs, databases, calculators, search engines - Requires well-defined interfaces - Enables precise operations model cannot perform
Agent: - Introduces autonomy through observe-think-act loops - Can plan, use tools, maintain memory - Pursues objectives through iterative reasoning - Enables adaptive, goal-directed behavior


32.3.3 System Layer Details
Pipeline: - Structures information flow through stages - Enables modularity and composability - Each stage independently testable - Provides observability into transformations
Evaluation: - Measures system behavior across distributions - Replaces deterministic testing with statistical evaluation - Provides signals for improvement - Essential for reliability and trust
Infrastructure: - Model serving, vector databases, caching - Provides operational foundation - Determines cost, latency, scalability - Enables deployment and monitoring
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.4 The Fundamental Transformation
AI engineering is defined by a fundamental transformation:
Traditional Software Engineering:
    ↓
Deterministic logic
Explicit instructions
Predictable behavior
Binary correctness

AI Engineering:
    ↓
Probabilistic inference
Structured prompts
Adaptive behavior
Statistical reliability
This transformation changes everything:

From functions to systems: Behavior emerges from component interactions
From implementation to orchestration: Focus shifts to system-level design
From testing to evaluation: Success measured across distributions
From predictability to reliability: Uncertainty managed through architecture
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.5 The Mental Model Shift
The transition to AI engineering requires a new mental model:

32.5.1 Old Mental Model (Function-Centric)
Input → Function → Output


32.5.2 New Mental Model (System-Centric)
System: Components → Interactions → Emergent Behavior
This shift changes how you approach problems:
Instead of writing a function to solve a problem directly, Design a pipeline that guides a model through transformations.
Instead of assuming correct code guarantees correct behavior, Build evaluation frameworks that continuously assess performance.
Instead of debugging by examining a single function, Trace information flow through multiple stages to identify failures.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.6 The Core Architectural Patterns
Three patterns recur throughout AI systems:

32.6.1 Pattern 1: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Query → Retrieval → Context Construction → Prompt → Model → Answer
Grounds responses in external knowledge, enables factual accuracy, extends model knowledge.


32.6.2 Pattern 2: Multi-Agent Collaboration
Coordinator → Specialists → Synthesis → Output
Leverages specialization, enables parallelization, handles complexity through division of labor.


32.6.3 Pattern 3: Iterative Refinement
Generate → Evaluate → Refine → Repeat
Improves outputs through feedback, enables self-correction, optimizes quality over iterations.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.7 The Design Principles
AI engineering is guided by core principles:

32.7.1 1. Systems Thinking
Think in terms of components and interactions, not isolated functions. Design architectures where behavior emerges from the system as a whole.


32.7.2 2. Structured Interfaces
Treat prompts as architectural components with clear contracts. Design interfaces that specify intent, context, constraints, and format.


32.7.3 3. Continuous Evaluation
Measure system behavior across distributions. Use evaluation as the primary mechanism for improvement and reliability.


32.7.4 4. Observability First
Design systems with visibility into internal behavior. Log prompts, traces, metrics, and decisions from the start.


32.7.5 5. Balanced Trade-offs
Optimize across cost, latency, and reliability. Recognize that improving one dimension often affects others.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.8 The Three Constraints
Every production AI system balances three fundamental constraints:
Cost
    ↓
Latency
    ↓
Reliability
Understanding these constraints is essential for practical AI engineering:
Cost: Model inference, infrastructure, storage, operations Latency: Model inference time, data retrieval, network communication Reliability: Accuracy, consistency, availability, robustness
Effective systems make conscious trade-offs across these constraints.
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.9 The Practical Framework
For actual implementation, the framework translates into concrete practices:

32.9.1 Design Phase

Define requirements and success criteria
Choose appropriate models for the task
Design prompt interfaces and templates
Plan retrieval and tool integration
Structure pipelines for information flow



32.9.2 Implementation Phase

Implement modular components
Build pipelines with clear stages
Add evaluation from the start
Instrument for observability
Handle errors gracefully



32.9.3 Optimization Phase

Evaluate across test datasets
Analyze failures systematically
Optimize prompts iteratively
Tune retrieval strategies
Balance cost, latency, reliability



32.9.4 Production Phase

Monitor system behavior continuously
Track costs and performance
Detect regressions early
Iterate based on feedback
Scale infrastructure as needed

text:
32.10 The Knowledge Hierarchy
AI engineering knowledge can be organized into levels:

32.10.1 Level 1: Understanding

What the AI engineering stack is
How probabilistic systems differ from deterministic ones
Why systems thinking is essential
The role of each component layer

How to coordinate agents

32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.11 The Core Questions
When faced with any AI engineering problem, ask these core questions:

32.11.1 About the Model Layer

What model capabilities are needed?
What data will the model require?
What are the model’s limitations?
How will the model be integrated?



32.11.2 About the Control Layer

How will prompts structure the interface?
What tools will extend capabilities?
Will agents add value through autonomy?
How will components coordinate?



32.11.3 About the System Layer

How will pipelines structure information flow?
How will evaluation ensure reliability?
What infrastructure is required?
How will the system scale?
crumbs: Part VII — Practical Guide to AI Engineering
32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.12 The Common Pitfalls
Avoid these common mistakes:

32.12.1 1. Over-Focusing on Models
Mistake: Choosing the most powerful model and assuming everything else will work.
Reality: Model choice is just one decision. Architecture matters more.


32.12.2 2. Treating Prompts as Text
Mistake: Writing prompts as if they’re just instructions to a human.
Reality: Prompts are architectural components requiring systematic design.


32.12.3 3. Skipping Evaluation
Mistake: Assuming the system works because it produces reasonable outputs.
Reality: Without systematic evaluation, you don’t know if improvements are real.


32.12.4 4. Ignoring Observability
Mistake: Deploying systems without visibility into internal behavior.
Reality: Opaque systems cannot be debugged, improved, or trusted.


32.12.5 5. Underestimating Infrastructure
Mistake: Assuming production is just “running it on a server.”
Reality: Infrastructure determines cost, latency, scalability, and reliability.

text:
32.13 The Future Evolution
As AI engineering evolves, the framework will expand:

32.13.1 Emerging Capabilities

More sophisticated multi-agent architectures
Automated prompt engineering
Self-improving systems
Cross-modal intelligence



32.13.2 Emerging Challenges

Scaling to billions of interactions
Ensuring safety and alignment
Managing ethical implications
Addressing societal impact



32.13.3 The Constant Core
Despite these changes, the core framework remains:

Models provide intelligence
Control layers direct that intelligence
System layers organize it into applications
Evaluation ensures reliability
Infrastructure enables operation

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.14 The Essential Takeaway
If you remember only one thing from this entire book, remember this:
AI engineers design systems that collaborate with intelligence.
This means:

Architectures matter more than algorithms
Interfaces matter more than implementations
Systems matter more than components
Evaluation matters more than testing

Every decision you make, every system you build, every problem you solve should be guided by this principle.

When building an AI system, verify:

32.15.1 Model Layer

Appropriate model selected for task

32.15.2 Control Layer

To master AI engineering, follow this path:

Understand the stack: Learn the three domains and eight layers

title: 32  Chapter 27 — Core Framework Recap: The AI Engineering Skeleton
text:
32.17 Conclusion
This chapter has presented the core framework that unifies everything in this book. The AI engineering stack—the three domains, eight layers, and their interactions—provides a mental model for thinking about, designing, and building intelligent systems.
Master this framework and you have mastered the essence of AI engineering. Everything else—specific techniques, tools, and technologies—fits within it.
The future of engineering is not just about writing code. It’s about designing systems that think, reason, and collaborate with us to achieve what neither humans nor machines could accomplish alone.
This is the promise of AI engineering. This is the framework that makes it possible.
Remember the stack. Build systems. Evaluate continuously. Iterate relentlessly.
This is the way of the AI engineer.

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.1 Introduction
Many developers think shell scripts are simply collections of commands. This is misleading.
A shell script is actually a program written in a command-oriented language designed to control processes and automate workflows.
Unlike general-purpose languages such as Python or Java, the shell language is primarily built around command execution and composition.
Understanding shell scripting requires seeing it as a language with: - Grammar - Control flow - Variables - Functions - Pipelines - Process management
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.2 The Execution Model
Every shell script follows the same execution model:
Script
   ↓
Shell Interpreter
   ↓
Command Parsing
   ↓
Process Execution
Example script:
#!/bin/bash

echo "Hello"
date
Execution steps: 1. The shell reads the script line by line 2. Each line is parsed 3. Commands are executed sequentially
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.4 The Grammar of Shell Scripts
Shell syntax revolves around commands and arguments. General form:
command arg1 arg2 arg3
Example:
ls -l /home
Structure:
command = ls
argument = -l
argument = /home
The shell splits input into tokens based on whitespace.
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.5 Variables
Variables store values that can be reused.
Example:
name="Alice"
echo $name
Important rule:
NO spaces around =
Correct:
name="Alice"
Incorrect:
name = "Alice"
To read a variable:
echo $name
or
echo ${name}
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.6 Command Substitution
Shell allows capturing the output of commands.
Example:
today=$(date)
echo $today
Execution:
date → output string → stored in variable
Older syntax:
today=`date`
But modern shells prefer $(...).
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.7 Control Flow

35.7.1 If Statements
Basic structure:
if condition
then
    command
fi
Example:
if [ -f file.txt ]
then
    echo "File exists"
fi
Here -f means “file exists.”
Other common tests:
-d  directory exists
-e  file exists
-z  string empty
-n  string not empty


35.7.2 If-Else
Example:
if [ -f file.txt ]
then
    echo "File exists"
else
    echo "File missing"
fi
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.8 Loops

35.8.1 For Loop
Example:
for file in *.txt
do
    echo $file
done
Execution:
find all .txt files
iterate over each file


35.8.2 While Loop
Example:
count=1

while [ $count -le 5 ]
do
    echo $count
    count=$((count+1))
done
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.9 Functions
Shell scripts can define functions.
Example:
greet() {
    echo "Hello $1"
}
Calling:
greet Alice
Output:
Hello Alice
Arguments:
$1 first argument
$2 second argument
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.10 Pipelines
Pipelines are one of the most powerful features of the shell.
Example:
ps aux | grep python
Execution model:
ps aux
   ↓
output pipe
   ↓
grep python
Each program reads input from the previous program. This design allows program composition.
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.11 Redirection
Shell also supports redirecting input and output.
Example:
echo "hello" > file.txt
Meaning:
write output to file
Append:
echo "hello" >> file.txt
Input redirection:
sort < file.txt
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.12 Process Control
Shell scripts often manage processes.
Running a command in the background:
python server.py &
The shell returns immediately.
List background jobs:
jobs
Bring a job to foreground:
fg
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.13 The Shell Programming Model
We can summarize shell scripting as a process orchestration language.
Variables
   ↓
Commands
   ↓
Pipelines
   ↓
Processes
   ↓
Output
Unlike traditional programming languages that focus on algorithms, shell scripts focus on coordinating programs.
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.14 The Conceptual Model
Shell scripts operate as a control layer above the operating system.
Script
   ↓
Shell
   ↓
Programs
   ↓
Operating System
The shell acts as a workflow controller.
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial
text:
35.15 Conclusion
Shell scripting is best understood not as a traditional programming language but as a process orchestration language.
Its strengths lie in: - Combining tools - Automating workflows - Managing processes - Interacting with the operating system
By mastering shell scripting, a developer gains direct control over the underlying system.
crumbs: Appendices
35  Appendix A — Shell Scripting Tutorial

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.1 Introduction
A command-line tool is not just a program that runs in the terminal. It is a structured command system that exposes operations through a textual interface.
Instead of buttons or menus, the interface consists of: - Commands - Subcommands - Flags - Arguments
Example:
Structure:
This structure is shared across almost all modern CLI tools.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
git commit -m "message"

command      → git
subcommand   → commit
flag         → -m
argument     → "message"
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.2 The Command Hierarchy
CLI tools typically follow a hierarchical command structure.
General model:
tool
 ├─ command
 │    ├─ subcommand
 │    └─ subcommand
 └─ command
Example:
git
 ├─ clone
 ├─ commit
 ├─ push
 ├─ pull
 └─ status
Another example:
docker
 ├─ build
 ├─ run
 ├─ push
 ├─ pull
 └─ ps
Each subcommand represents a specific operation.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.3 The Command Pattern
A CLI tool usually follows this execution pattern:
input command
     ↓
argument parsing
     ↓
command dispatch
     ↓
execute function
Example:
git status
Execution flow:
git program starts
   ↓
parse arguments
   ↓
detect command "status"
   ↓
call status handler
This is essentially command routing.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.4 Arguments and Flags
CLI tools accept additional parameters. These are typically categorized as: - Flags - Options - Positional arguments
Example:
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx
Breakdown:
command    → run
flag       → -p
argument   → 8080:80
image      → nginx
Flags modify behavior.
Example:
git log --oneline

36.4.1 Short vs Long Flags
Flags often have two forms.
Short form:
-m
Long form:
--message
Example:
git commit -m "hello"
git commit --message "hello"
This improves usability.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.5 Parsing the Command
Before executing anything, the CLI tool must parse the command line.
Input string:
git commit -m "hello"
Parsed representation:
program: git
command: commit
flags:
   message: hello
Internally this becomes something like:
Command {
    name: "commit"
    options: {
        message: "hello"
    }
}
Many languages provide libraries for this.
Python:
argparse
click
typer
Go:
cobra
urfave/cli
Node:
commander
yargs
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.6 Command Dispatch
Once the command is parsed, the CLI tool must dispatch the correct handler.
Example architecture:
commands
 ├─ commit()
 ├─ push()
 ├─ pull()
 └─ status()
Dispatcher:
if command == "commit":
    run_commit()
elif command == "push":
    run_push()
More advanced systems use command registries.

36.6.1 Example CLI Architecture
Typical project structure:
cli-tool/
│
├─ main
│
├─ commands/
│   ├─ build
│   ├─ run
│   └─ deploy
│
└─ internal/
    └─ core logic
Entry point:
main
  ↓
command parser
  ↓
command dispatcher
  ↓
handler
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.7 CLI as an Interface Layer
A CLI tool is essentially an interface to a system.
Example:
git CLI
   ↓
git core engine
   ↓
filesystem operations
Another example:
docker CLI
   ↓
docker daemon
   ↓
container runtime
In many cases the CLI is simply a client.

36.7.1 CLI as an API
This means CLI commands function like an API.
Example:
git commit
Equivalent conceptual call:
commit(repository, message)
CLI tools provide a text-based API to system operations.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.8 The CLI Tool Pattern
Most CLI tools share a common architecture pattern:
CLI Tool
│
├─ command parser
├─ command registry
├─ command handlers
└─ system interface
Execution flow:
User command
     ↓
Parser
     ↓
Dispatcher
     ↓
Handler
     ↓
System action
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.9 CLI Design Principles
Good CLI tools follow several design principles.

36.9.1 Consistency
Commands should follow predictable patterns.
Example:
git add
git commit
git push


36.9.2 Discoverability
Users should easily explore commands.
Example:
git help
git --help


36.9.3 Composability
CLI tools should work with other tools.
Example:
git log | grep bug


36.9.4 Scriptability
Commands should be automatable.
Example:
for repo in repos/*
do
    git pull
done
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.10 The CLI Ecosystem
Modern developer environments consist largely of CLI tools.
Example stack:
git
docker
kubectl
npm
terraform
gh
iflow
Each tool provides a command interface to a complex system.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.11 The Deeper Insight
CLI tools are essentially structured command languages.
They transform:
text input
     ↓
structured command
     ↓
system operation
This pattern is similar to: - SQL - HTTP APIs - GraphQL
The CLI is simply another interaction protocol.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns

title: 36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
text:
36.12 Conclusion
Command-line tools are best understood as structured interfaces to systems.
They provide: - Command hierarchies - Argument parsing - Command dispatch - System execution
Once understood this way, CLI tools become much easier to design and extend.
crumbs: Appendices
36  Appendix B — CLI Tool Design Patterns
